stem:
A 21-year-old university student is brought to a community psychiatric assessment service by his parents with a three week history of paranoid ideation, persecutory beliefs about being followed by a state intelligence agency, and second person auditory hallucinations criticising him. He has been using high potency cannabis daily for the past four months around exam stress. He has used no other illicit substances on urine drug screen and serology.

There is no history of head injury, neurological disease, or thyroid disorder. He has a maternal uncle with chronic schizophrenia. He has stopped attending lectures and his sleep is reversed. Mental state examination shows a tense young man, suspicious, with thought disorder including loosening of associations. He has no insight. The parents say he was 'completely well' four months ago and his GP letter confirms no prior psychiatric contact.

data_table.Investigations: urine drug screen positive for cannabinoids only, full blood count within normal limits, thyroid function within normal limits, CRP 2 mg/L, syphilis serology negative, HIV serology negative, vitamin B12 and folate within normal limits, computed tomography of the brain within normal limits

lead_in: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Diagnose cannabis induced psychotic disorder, prescribe risperidone 1 mg twice daily for five days, discharge to GP review
options.rationale: Even if cannabis induced psychosis is the working diagnosis, three weeks of fixed persecutory beliefs, second person auditory hallucinations, and thought disorder in a 21-year-old with family history warrant assertive specialist follow up rather than GP review. Premature discharge risks treatment dropout and decompensation. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline for Schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline for Schizophrenia

options.text: Apply for an involuntary admission order under the Mental Health Act without offering voluntary admission first to him
options.rationale: Voluntary admission should be offered first if the patient has capacity and agrees. Involuntary admission is reserved for refusal in a person at risk of harm to self or others. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline for Schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline for Schizophrenia

options.text: Commence high dose oral olanzapine 20 mg daily and arrange clozapine clinic review at four weeks for resistant symptoms
options.rationale: Clozapine is reserved for treatment resistance (failure of two adequate trials of other antipsychotics). High dose olanzapine in a stimulant naive young man exposes him to weight gain, metabolic syndrome, and sedation. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline for Schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline for Schizophrenia

options.text: Admit voluntarily under early psychosis service for trial of an oral second generation antipsychotic and substance counselling
options.rationale: First episode psychosis with persistent positive symptoms beyond a few days of abstinence cannot be reliably labelled cannabis induced until at least one month of cannabis abstinence shows symptom resolution. Early Psychosis Prevention and Intervention Centre (EPPIC) model favours assertive specialist follow up, low dose oral second generation antipsychotic, and integrated substance use treatment. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline for Schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline for Schizophrenia

options.text: Defer pharmacotherapy and observe abstinent in a residential rehabilitation unit for two weeks before any antipsychotic
options.rationale: Three weeks of psychotic symptoms after presentation already exceeds the two week abstinence window that would support a substance induced diagnosis. Withholding antipsychotic medication delays symptom resolution. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline for Schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline for Schizophrenia

explanation.summary: Differentiating substance induced from primary psychosis in a young person with cannabis use and a positive family history is clinically difficult and often requires longitudinal observation. The RANZCP and Orygen early psychosis pathway favours treating as first episode psychosis if symptoms persist beyond a month of abstinence (or earlier if a clear primary disorder is suspected) using low dose oral second generation antipsychotic, integrated substance use treatment, and assertive case management. About one in three cannabis induced psychoses convert to schizophrenia within three years.
key_points: Persistence of psychosis beyond one month of cannabis abstinence supports primary psychosis
Around 30 percent of cannabis induced psychoses convert to schizophrenia within 3 years
Low dose oral second generation antipsychotic is first line (e.g. risperidone, aripiprazole, olanzapine)
Integrated substance treatment within early psychosis service
Family history of schizophrenia roughly doubles conversion risk
explanation.pearls: Among cannabis users with a first episode psychosis, around 30 percent meet criteria for schizophrenia within three years on Australian early psychosis cohort follow up - assertive engagement during this window predicts long term function.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline for Schizophrenia

stem:
A 48-year-old woman attends general practice for assistance with quitting smoking. She has smoked 25 cigarettes per day for 30 years, has had three previous unaided quit attempts that lasted between two days and three weeks, and her Heaviness of Smoking Index is 6. She has had two depressive episodes in the past treated with sertraline, the last episode resolving 18 months ago. She has stable hypertension on perindopril.

She has had one generalised tonic-clonic seizure at age 19 attributed to alcohol withdrawal at the time and has not had another. She is not pregnant. She has tried 21 mg nicotine patches in the past without success and dislikes nicotine gum. She has commercial driver's licence requirements at work. She is keen to use the most effective option supported by Australian guidelines and has heard about varenicline.

data_table.Investigations: blood pressure 142/86, weight 78 kg, full blood count and liver function within normal limits, urinary pregnancy test negative, ECG within normal limits

lead_in: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence varenicline 0.5 mg daily for 3 days, 0.5 mg twice daily for 4 days, then 1 mg twice daily for 12 weeks
options.rationale: Varenicline is the most effective monotherapy for smoking cessation (number needed to treat about 8 versus placebo at 12 months) and is first line in Australia. Past depression treated and resolved is not a contraindication; previous concerns about neuropsychiatric harm were not borne out by the EAGLES trial. (Source: RACGP: Supporting Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Commence bupropion sustained release 150 mg daily for 3 days then 150 mg twice daily for 7 to 12 weeks as monotherapy
options.rationale: Bupropion is contraindicated by prior seizure (even alcohol withdrawal seizure historically) because it lowers the seizure threshold. It is also less effective than varenicline and combination NRT. (Source: RACGP: Supporting Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Re-prescribe 21 mg nicotine patches as sole therapy and reassure that previous failure does not predict future failure
options.rationale: Single agent patches at high dependence have lower efficacy than combination NRT (patch plus short acting form) or varenicline. Repeating a previously failed strategy without intensifying is unlikely to succeed. (Source: RACGP: Supporting Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Refer for nicotine vaping as a harm reduction strategy and avoid any prescription smoking cessation pharmacotherapy
options.rationale: Nicotine vaping is a second line option in Australia under prescription where first line agents have failed or are not suitable. It is not first line and is not preferred over varenicline. (Source: RACGP: Supporting Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Recommend an Allen Carr style behavioural change program alone without any pharmacotherapy and review in three months
options.rationale: Behavioural support is encouraged but works best in combination with pharmacotherapy in highly dependent smokers (Heaviness of Smoking Index 5 or more). Withholding pharmacotherapy underuses an evidence based intervention. (Source: RACGP: Supporting Smoking Cessation)

explanation.summary: Australian smoking cessation pharmacotherapy is led by varenicline (number needed to treat about 8 versus placebo at 12 months), followed by combination nicotine replacement therapy (patch plus short acting), then monotherapy NRT, then bupropion. Behavioural support plus pharmacotherapy roughly doubles cessation rates compared with either alone. Bupropion is contraindicated by seizure history, current or past eating disorder, MAOI use within two weeks, and severe hepatic impairment. Nicotine vaping is second line under prescription where first line agents have failed or are not suitable.
key_points: Varenicline first line - NNT ~8 at 12 months
Combination NRT (patch + short acting) second line (NNT ~11)
Bupropion third line - contraindicated by seizures, eating disorders, MAOI
Past resolved depression NOT a contraindication to varenicline (EAGLES trial)
Behavioural support plus pharmacotherapy roughly doubles cessation
explanation.pearls: EAGLES trial (4 arm RCT in 8000 smokers with and without psychiatric history) found no excess of neuropsychiatric adverse events with varenicline compared with placebo, patch, or bupropion - past depression alone is no longer a contraindication.

subtopic_detail: Schizoaffective versus bipolar with psychotic features
stem:
A 32-year-old woman is admitted to an acute psychiatric ward in her fourth presentation in five years. The current episode features one month of elevated mood, reduced need for sleep, grandiose delusions of being a celebrity, and pressure of speech, with persecutory voices saying she will be killed. The euphoria resolved with oral olanzapine 20 mg and lithium 1 g daily over 10 days but the persecutory voices persist three weeks after mood symptoms have settled.

Review of her records shows two previous mood episodes (one manic, one major depressive) and one inter-episode period of four months when she had paranoid delusions and second person hallucinations in the absence of any mood symptoms. She is medication adherent. There is no substance misuse. Cognition is intact. She is currently euthymic on mental state examination and her current PANSS shows persistent positive symptoms.

data_table.Investigations: blood lithium level 0.8 mmol/L, full blood count and thyroid function within normal limits, urinary drug screen negative, CT brain within normal limits

lead_in: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Paranoid schizophrenia with secondary mood symptoms from psychotic decompensation
options.rationale: Schizophrenia is diagnosed when mood symptoms are present for a minority of the active and residual illness, which is not the case in someone with two prior major mood episodes plus the current manic episode. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders)

options.text: Bipolar I disorder with mood congruent psychotic features during the manic episode
options.rationale: Bipolar I with psychotic features requires that psychotic symptoms occur only during mood episodes. The documented four month inter-episode psychotic period rules this out. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders)

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder, bipolar type, with persistent inter-episode psychotic symptoms
options.rationale: The DSM-5 criterion for schizoaffective disorder requires a major mood episode concurrent with criterion A schizophrenia symptoms, plus at least two weeks of psychotic symptoms in the absence of mood symptoms during the lifetime course. The four month period of psychosis without mood symptoms satisfies this. (Source: RANZCP Schizoaffective Disorder)

options.text: Delusional disorder persecutory type with concurrent bipolar I and current manic episode
options.rationale: Delusional disorder requires non-bizarre delusions without prominent hallucinations and minimal functional impairment outside the delusion - inconsistent with the prominent hallucinations and functional decline here. Comorbidity with bipolar I is rarely diagnosed and would not capture the cross sectional picture. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders)

options.text: Substance induced psychotic and mood disorder despite negative urinary drug screen
options.rationale: Negative urinary drug screen and absence of substance misuse history exclude a substance induced disorder. The longitudinal pattern across five years is incompatible with intoxication or withdrawal phenomena. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders)

explanation.summary: Schizoaffective disorder requires (a) a major mood episode concurrent with schizophrenia criterion A symptoms, (b) at least two weeks of psychotic symptoms in the absence of mood symptoms at some time in the lifetime course, and (c) mood symptoms present for a majority of the active and residual illness. The 'bipolar type' subtype is given when manic episodes have occurred, the 'depressive type' when only major depressive episodes have occurred. Treatment combines mood stabiliser (lithium or sodium valproate) with an antipsychotic and structured psychosocial care; clozapine is considered for treatment resistance.
key_points: Schizoaffective: major mood episode plus criterion A schizophrenia, plus 2 or more weeks of psychosis without mood
Bipolar with psychotic features: psychosis only during mood episodes
Schizophrenia: mood symptoms are a minority of active/residual illness
Treatment: mood stabiliser plus antipsychotic; clozapine for treatment resistance
Diagnosis can shift across the course; longitudinal history is essential
explanation.pearls: Two weeks of psychotic symptoms in the absence of any mood episode at any point in the lifetime course is the boundary criterion that distinguishes schizoaffective disorder from bipolar I with psychotic features - mine the chart for inter-episode periods to establish this.

stem:
A 56-year-old man with bipolar I disorder has been on lithium 1000 mg daily for 22 years. He is psychiatrically well, has had no manic episodes in 12 years on lithium plus aripiprazole 5 mg, and is followed in shared care between psychiatry and general practice. He has hypertension on perindopril (added two years ago) and no diabetes. He drinks one glass of red wine four nights a week. His eGFR has been monitored every six months.

The most recent eGFR is 38 mL/min/1.73 m^2, falling from 54 a year ago and 72 two years ago. Lithium level today is 0.9 mmol/L. Thyroid stimulating hormone is 4.2 mU/L. Corrected calcium is 2.62 mmol/L (2.10-2.60). 24 hour urinary protein is 0.4 g (less than 0.15). He has no symptoms of polyuria, polydipsia, or tremor. The renal physician you spoke with suggested coming off lithium would not necessarily reverse the decline.

data_table.Investigations: lithium 0.9 mmol/L, eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73 m^2 declining from 54 then 72, thyroid stimulating hormone 4.2 mU/L, corrected calcium 2.62 mmol/L, parathyroid hormone 9.4 pmol/L (1.6-6.9), 24 hour urinary protein 0.4 g, blood pressure 138/82, urinary drug screen not performed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue lithium 1000 mg daily and recheck eGFR in six months without specialist renal medicine input
options.rationale: Progressive eGFR decline through stage 3b CKD on a known nephrotoxin warrants psychiatric and renal multidisciplinary review with a documented plan to switch or taper, not passive monitoring. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar Guideline)

options.text: Halve the lithium dose and target a serum level of 0.4 mmol/L to reduce nephrotoxicity over the next year
options.rationale: Dose reduction below 0.6 mmol/L generally loses prophylactic efficacy and does not reliably halt renal decline. Multidisciplinary planning toward alternative agents is preferred. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar Guideline)

options.text: Stop lithium today to halt further renal damage and substitute high dose sodium valproate from tomorrow morning
options.rationale: Abrupt cessation of lithium after long term use carries up to 50 percent risk of mood relapse within 12 months. A planned slow taper is preferred. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar Guideline)

options.text: Increase fluid intake to four litres daily and continue lithium at the same dose without specialist referral
options.rationale: Volume loading does not reverse established chronic interstitial nephritis from lithium and may risk hyponatraemia. The dose should not stay the same in CKD stage 3b without weighing alternatives. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar Guideline)

options.text: Coordinate joint psychiatry and renal medicine review for a slow lithium taper to sodium valproate or lamotrigine
options.rationale: Australian psychiatric guidance advises planned multidisciplinary review when eGFR falls under 60 and especially under 45, weighing relapse risk against renal benefit; a slow taper over 3 to 6 months with an alternative mood stabiliser is preferred. Abrupt cessation risks relapse and may not reverse renal decline beyond stage 3b. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar Guideline)

explanation.summary: Long term lithium use carries cumulative renal risk through chronic tubulointerstitial nephritis and nephrogenic diabetes insipidus. Surveillance every six months should include eGFR, lithium level, thyroid stimulating hormone, calcium and parathyroid hormone (lithium induced hyperparathyroidism), and urinalysis. eGFR below 60 prompts shared care with renal medicine; below 45 prompts active discussion of cessation versus continuation, weighing 50 percent twelve month relapse risk if stopped abruptly. Lithium induced hyperparathyroidism is treated by lithium cessation where possible or parathyroidectomy.
key_points: 6 monthly: eGFR, Li level, TSH, calcium/PTH, urinalysis
eGFR < 60: shared care with renal medicine
eGFR < 45: actively discuss cessation versus continuation
Slow taper over 3 to 6 months reduces relapse risk
Lithium induced hyperparathyroidism: high calcium with high or inappropriately normal PTH
explanation.pearls: Hypercalcaemia plus inappropriately normal or high PTH in a patient on long term lithium is lithium induced hyperparathyroidism - around 10 percent prevalence after 10 years of lithium therapy, and the parathyroid hyperplasia often does not reverse on lithium withdrawal.

stem:
A 19-year-old man with a six month prodromal history of social withdrawal and odd beliefs is brought to a psychiatric assessment unit by his family. Over the past five days he has stopped eating and drinking, has been mute, and adopts unusual postures for hours at a time. He has not been on any medication in the past year. He has no fever, no rigidity, and no autonomic instability. Vital signs are within normal limits.

On examination he is awake with eyes open but unresponsive to verbal commands. Posturing is present (he holds his arm in the air when placed there for at least 30 seconds). There is waxy flexibility on passive movement, mutism, negativism (resists eye opening), and grimacing. The remainder of the neurological examination cannot be completed due to negativism. There is no neck stiffness. Computed tomography of the brain and a metabolic screen are unremarkable.

data_table.Investigations: venous blood gas pH 7.42, sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.0 mmol/L, urea 4.6 mmol/L, creatine kinase 142 U/L (<200), thyroid stimulating hormone within normal limits, urinary drug screen negative, computed tomography of the brain within normal limits, urinalysis sterile

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence intravenous haloperidol 5 mg every 6 hours to treat the underlying suspected schizophrenic psychosis directly
options.rationale: Antipsychotics are relatively contraindicated as first line in catatonia because of the risk of precipitating neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) and worsening catatonic features. Benzodiazepines are first line. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Guideline)

options.text: Lorazepam 2 mg intramuscular bolus and reassess catatonic features at 15 minutes, then oral lorazepam 6 to 12 mg daily
options.rationale: Lorazepam 1 to 2 mg IV or IM is both diagnostic and therapeutic in catatonia: around 70 percent of cases show measurable improvement in Bush Francis catatonia rating score within 15 to 30 minutes. Lack of response prompts urgent consideration of electroconvulsive therapy. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Guideline)

options.text: Arrange urgent electroconvulsive therapy under general anaesthesia without trial of pharmacotherapy as the first step
options.rationale: ECT is second line after lorazepam (and may be first line in life-threatening malignant catatonia with autonomic instability), but trialling lorazepam first is standard in non-malignant catatonia. ECT is also indicated when there is no response within 24 to 48 hours. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Guideline)

options.text: Commence intravenous flumazenil 200 micrograms to exclude benzodiazepine intoxication as a cause of unresponsiveness
options.rationale: Flumazenil is contraindicated when a seizure risk exists, may precipitate seizures, and addresses a different clinical picture from catatonia. The urinary drug screen excludes benzodiazepine intoxication here. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Guideline)

options.text: Commence intravenous dantrolene 2 mg/kg to treat presumed neuroleptic malignant syndrome with rigidity and autonomic signs
options.rationale: NMS requires recent exposure to dopamine antagonists, hyperthermia, rigidity, autonomic instability, and a raised creatine kinase - none of which are present here. Dantrolene has no role in non-malignant catatonia. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Guideline)

explanation.summary: Catatonia is a clinically diagnosed syndrome (Bush Francis Catatonia Rating Scale: 3 or more of 14 features) that complicates schizophrenia, mood disorders, and a range of medical conditions including autoimmune encephalitis. First line treatment is the lorazepam challenge (1 to 2 mg IV or IM with assessment at 15 minutes); around 70 percent improve dramatically. If lorazepam fails or malignant catatonia develops (hyperthermia, autonomic instability), ECT is the next step. Antipsychotics are relatively contraindicated because they may precipitate NMS. Always look for an underlying cause: autoimmune encephalitis (NMDA receptor), HIV, encephalitis, lupus.
key_points: Bush Francis Catatonia Rating Scale: 3 or more of 14 features
Lorazepam 1 to 2 mg IV or IM (diagnostic and therapeutic - 70 percent response)
ECT for refractory catatonia or malignant catatonia
Avoid antipsychotics first line (precipitate NMS)
Screen for autoimmune (NMDA receptor encephalitis), HIV, lupus, metabolic
explanation.pearls: Malignant catatonia (catatonia with hyperthermia, autonomic instability, and rigidity) is clinically indistinguishable from neuroleptic malignant syndrome - the differentiator is dopamine antagonist exposure history; treatment is electroconvulsive therapy and supportive ICU care regardless.

stem:
A 62-year-old man with a bioprosthetic aortic valve replacement four months ago is admitted to a tertiary cardiology unit with two weeks of fevers, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss of 3 kg. He had a dental extraction six weeks ago without antibiotic prophylaxis. He has no penicillin allergy and no intravenous drug use.

On examination he is alert but unwell-appearing. He has new conjunctival petechiae and three small painless lesions on the palms (Janeway lesions). Auscultation reveals an early diastolic murmur at the left sternal edge with a soft heart sound at the base. Capillary return is two seconds. Three separate sets of blood cultures over two hours have been collected. Transthoracic echocardiogram shows a 9 mm vegetation on the aortic prosthesis with moderate aortic regurgitation but no abscess. His vital signs are below.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 102, blood pressure 124/64, respiratory rate 18, SpO2 97 percent on room air, temperature 38.9 degrees
data_table.Investigations: haemoglobin 102 g/L, white cells 14.2 x10^9/L, CRP 162 mg/L, creatinine 88 micromol/L, three sets of blood cultures pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin 1.8 g every 4 hours plus gentamicin 1 mg/kg every 8 hours for empirical native valve cover
options.rationale: Benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin is appropriate for empirical native valve endocarditis with subacute presentation but does not cover methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus or coagulase negative staphylococci, both of which are common in early prosthetic valve endocarditis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous flucloxacillin 2 g every 4 hours as monotherapy targeting methicillin sensitive staphylococcus aureus only
options.rationale: Flucloxacillin covers methicillin sensitive Staphylococcus aureus but misses MRSA, coagulase negative staphylococci, and lacks biofilm penetration appropriate for prosthetic valve endocarditis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin 25 mg/kg load then 15 to 20 mg/kg twice daily, gentamicin 1 mg/kg every 8 hours, plus rifampicin
options.rationale: Prosthetic valve endocarditis under 12 months from surgery is treated empirically as for staphylococci including MRSA, with vancomycin plus gentamicin plus rifampicin to penetrate biofilm. Rifampicin is added once stable on vancomycin/gentamicin for biofilm activity. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone 2 g daily plus oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for empirical Q fever endocarditis cover
options.rationale: Q fever endocarditis is unusual outside livestock contact and typically presents with culture negative endocarditis; empirical cover for the common pathogens of prosthetic valve endocarditis is the priority while serology is pending. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g every 8 hours as broad spectrum empirical cover until culture results return
options.rationale: Piperacillin-tazobactam is appropriate for nosocomial sepsis but does not cover MRSA or coagulase negative staphylococci which dominate early prosthetic valve endocarditis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Prosthetic valve endocarditis (PVE) within 12 months of surgery is dominated by Staphylococcus aureus and coagulase negative staphylococci, often methicillin resistant, with biofilm formation on the prosthesis. Australian empirical therapy is vancomycin plus gentamicin plus rifampicin. Late PVE (over 12 months) and native valve endocarditis follow a different empirical regimen (benzylpenicillin plus flucloxacillin plus gentamicin). Surgery is indicated for heart failure, uncontrolled infection, large vegetation greater than 10 mm with embolism, perivalvular abscess, or fungal endocarditis.
key_points: Early PVE (< 12 months): vancomycin + gentamicin + rifampicin
Late PVE and native valve: benzylpenicillin + flucloxacillin + gentamicin
Modified Duke criteria for diagnosis
TOE more sensitive than TTE; mandatory for PVE
Surgical indications: heart failure, uncontrolled infection, abscess, large vegetation with embolism
explanation.pearls: Rifampicin must NEVER be started before two negative blood cultures on vancomycin or daptomycin - introducing rifampicin into a high inoculum staphylococcal bacteraemia rapidly selects rifampicin-resistant mutants and renders the biofilm-active drug useless.
